content:
# Acts Chapter 26 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 257-258 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_026_page_001.jpg, ACT_026_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And Agrippa said to Paul: "It-is-permitted for-you to-speak concerning yourself." Then Paul, having-stretched-out the hand, was-making-defense:

**2.** "Concerning all-things of-which I-am-accused by [the] Jews, king Agrippa, I-have-considered myself blessed being-about to-make-defense before you today,

**3.** especially [because you are] a-knower of all the customs and also questions among [the] Jews; therefore I-beg you to-hear me patiently.

**4.** The manner-of-life of-me therefore from youth, the [one] having-been from [the] beginning among my nation and in Jerusalem, all the Jews know,

**5.** knowing-me-beforehand from [the] first, if they-would-be-willing to-testify, that according-to the strictest sect of our religion I-lived [as] a Pharisee.

**6.** And now upon [the] hope of-the promise made by God to our fathers I-have-stood being-judged,

**7.** to which our twelve-tribes in-earnestness night and day serving hope to-attain; concerning which hope I-am-accused by [the] Jews, O-king.

**8.** Why [is it] judged unbelievable among you if God raises [the] dead?

**9.** I therefore thought to-myself [that] against the name of-Jesus the Nazorean it-was-necessary to-do many-things contrary,

**10.** which also I-did in Jerusalem, and many of-the saints I shut-up in prisons, having-received the authority from the chief-priests, and [when] they-were-being-put-to-death I-cast-down a-vote [against them],

**11.** and throughout all the synagogues many-times punishing them, I-was-compelling [them] to-blaspheme, and exceedingly being-enraged against them, I-was-persecuting [them] even unto the cities outside.

**12.** In which-things [while] going to Damascus with authority and commission from the chief-priests,

**13.** at-midday along the way I-saw, O-king, from-heaven above the brightness of-the sun, having-shone-around me a-light and the-ones with me traveling-together.

**14.** And all-of-us having-fallen-down to the ground, I-heard a-voice saying to me in the Hebrew language: 'Saoul, Saoul, why are-you-persecuting me? [It is] hard for-you to-kick against goads.'

**15.** And I said: 'Who are-you, Lord?' And the Lord said: 'I am Jesus whom you are-persecuting.

**16.** But rise-up and stand upon your feet; for unto this I-appeared to-you, to-appoint you [as] a-servant and a-witness both of-the-things [in] which you-saw me and of-the-things [in] which I-shall-appear to-you,

**17.** delivering you from the people and from the nations, to whom I am-sending you,

**18.** to-open [the] eyes of-them, to-turn [them] from darkness to light and [from] the authority of Satan to God, [for them] to-receive forgiveness of-sins and a-lot among the-ones having-been-sanctified by faith the [one] in me.'

**19.** From-which-thing, O-king Agrippa, I-was not disobedient to-the heavenly vision,

**20.** but to-the-ones in Damascus first and [then] in Jerusalem and all the region of Judea, and to-the nations, I-was-declaring to-repent and to-turn to God, doing works worthy of-repentance.

**21.** On-account-of these-things [the] Jews, having-seized me in the temple, were-attempting to-kill [me with their own]-hands.

**22.** Having-obtained therefore help from God, until this day I-have-stood witnessing both to-small and to-great, saying nothing outside of-the-things which both the prophets and Moses said were-about to-happen,

**23.** whether the Christ [was] subject-to-suffering, whether [as] first out-of [the] resurrection of [the] dead he-is-about to-proclaim light both to-the people and to-the nations."

**24.** And [while] he [was] making-defense with these-things, Festus with-a-loud voice says: "You-are-mad, Paul! The many writings are-turning you to madness."

**25.** But Paul says: "I-am not mad, most-excellent Festus, but words of-truth and of-soundness-of-mind I-am-uttering.

**26.** For the king knows concerning these-things, to whom also speaking-boldly I-speak; for to-have-escaped-notice [of] him any of these-things I-am not persuaded, for this is not having-been-done in a-corner.

**27.** Do-you-believe, king Agrippa, the prophets? I-know that you-believe."

**28.** And Agrippa [said] to Paul: "In a-little [time] you-are-persuading me to-make [me] a-Christian."

**29.** And Paul [said]: "I-would-pray to God, both in a-little and in a-great [time], not only you but also all the-ones hearing me today to-become such-as also I am, except [for] these bonds."

**30.** And the king rose-up, and the governor, and Bernice, and the-ones sitting-with them,

**31.** and having-withdrawn they-were-speaking to one-another saying: "Nothing worthy of-death or of-bonds is this man doing."

**32.** And Agrippa said to-Festus: "This man was-able to-have-been-released, if he-had not appealed-to Caesar."

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus, pages 257-258)

1. **Verse 1 -- ΕΚΤΕΙΝΑΣ ΤΗΝ ΧΕΙΡΑ:** Sinaiticus reads "the hand" (singular), consistent with the formal gesture of defense. Some later manuscripts add "αὐτοῦ" (his), but Sinaiticus omits the possessive pronoun.

2. **Verse 7 -- ΔΩΔΕΚΑΦΥΛΟΝ:** The compound word "twelve-tribe" (δωδεκάφυλον) is rendered as a single concept. Sinaiticus preserves this as one word, referring to the entirety of Israel's tribal heritage. This is a unique NT term appearing only here.

3. **Verse 8:** The rhetorical question uses εἰ with the indicative (ΕΙ Ο ΘΕΟΣ ΝΕΚΡΟΥΣ ΕΓΕΙΡΕΙ), presenting the resurrection as a real condition rather than a hypothetical. This is significant for Paul's argument -- he treats the resurrection of the dead as factual.

4. **Verse 9 -- ΝΑΖΩΡΑΙΟΥ:** Sinaiticus reads "Nazorean" (Ναζωραῖου), not "Nazarene" (Ναζαρηνοῦ). These are distinct forms in the manuscript tradition; "Nazorean" is the form consistently used in Acts.

5. **Verse 10 -- ΚΑΤΗΝΕΓΚΑ ΨΗΦΟΝ:** Literally "I-cast-down a-vote/pebble." The word ψῆφον refers to a voting pebble, suggesting Paul had formal juridical authority in the condemnation of Christians, possibly as a member of the Sanhedrin.

6. **Verse 14 -- ΣΑΟΥΛ ΣΑΟΥΛ:** Sinaiticus preserves the Semitic form of the name (Σαούλ) and explicitly states the voice spoke "in the Hebrew language" (τῇ Ἑβραΐδι διαλέκτῳ). This detail appears only in the Acts 26 account, not in chapters 9 or 22. The proverb about kicking against goads (πρὸς κέντρα λακτίζειν) also appears only in this account.

7. **Verse 17 -- ΕΞΑΙΡΟΥΜΕΝΟΣ:** This participle can mean either "delivering/rescuing" or "choosing/selecting." In context, "delivering" is preferred, as it echoes God's protective action over Paul's mission.

8. **Verse 20 -- ΧΩΡΑΝ ΤΗΣ ΙΟΥΔΑΙΑΣ:** Sinaiticus reads "region of Judea." Some witnesses read πᾶσάν τε τὴν χώραν ("and all the region"), which Sinaiticus appears to support.

9. **Verse 23 -- ΕΙ ΠΑΘΗΤΟΣ Ο ΧΡΙΣΤΟΣ:** The adjective παθητός ("subject to suffering") is a verbal adjective unique to this passage in the NT. It presents the Messiah's capacity for suffering as a theological proposition drawn from the prophets.

10. **Verse 24 -- ΤΑ ΠΟΛΛΑ ΣΕ ΓΡΑΜΜΑΤΑ:** Literally "the many writings/letters." Festus attributes Paul's passionate speech to excessive learning (γράμματα), not to scripture specifically. The term is ambiguous -- it could mean "learning" broadly or "letters/scriptures."

11. **Verse 28 -- ΕΝ ΟΛΙΓΩ:** Sinaiticus reads ἐν ὀλίγῳ ("in a little"). This famous verse has been much debated. The phrase may mean "in a short [time]," "with little [effort]," or "in brief [argument]." I have preserved the ambiguity with "in a-little." The Byzantine tradition's reading "almost" (which produced the KJV "Almost thou persuadest me") is an interpretive expansion not found in Sinaiticus.

12. **Verse 29 -- ΚΑΙ ΕΝ ΟΛΙΓΩ ΚΑΙ ΕΝ ΜΕΓΑΛΩ:** Paul's response mirrors Agrippa's phrase and extends it: "both in a little and in a great [time/measure]." This rhetorical echo is elegant in the Greek and must be preserved in translation.

13. **Verse 32 -- ΑΠΟΛΕΛΥΣΘΑΙ:** The perfect passive infinitive "to-have-been-released" emphasizes the completed state of potential freedom now foreclosed by Paul's appeal. Sinaiticus is clear on this reading.

14. **General variant:** Throughout this chapter, Sinaiticus shows no significant lacunae or scribal corrections in the main text of Acts 26. The text is well-preserved and legible across both folios.

-- EMET
